description: This test case evaluates the system's ability to retrieve and synthesize information from multiple, separate document sections to answer a complex question.
question: How does wafer orientation affect NBTI degradation in p-MOSFETs, and what measurement techniques can be used to characterize NBTI degradation while minimizing measurement artifacts?

gold_context_sources.evidence: Most ICs are fabricated on (100) oriented Si wafers. However, it has been known since 1968 that hole mobility is higher for p-MOSFETs on (110) oriented wafers with the channel in the (110) direction. Along with the higher hole mobility, however, is a higher interface trap density. Dy has been reported as D;(100)=7.7x 10" and Dy(110)=14x10"cm2eV™'. Hence one would expect more severe NBTI degradation on (110) oriented wafers, as indeed has been observed, as shown in Fig. 11. This is a potential problem if (110) oriented wafers become important. For certain three-dimensional devices, e.g., FinFETs, when fabricated on (100) wafers with the channel in the conventional (110) direction, the vertical sidewalls are (110) oriented, leading to NBTI problems. However, forming FinFETs on (100) wafers with the channel in the (100) direction, leads to (100) vertical sidewalls.

Traditionally NBTI has been characterized by stressing a device, then interrupting the stress to measure a device parameter, e.g., VT, ID, leﬀ, Dit, Not, stress again, measure, etc. VT, ID, and leﬀ are determined from ID–VD and ID–VG measurements. The interface trap density Dit is usually measured with charge pumping or log(ID)–VG sub-threshold swing measurements. Usually source, drain and substrate are grounded during the stress period. The ‘‘dead’’ time between ‘‘stress’’ and ‘‘measure’’ varied from researcher to researcher and was frequently not given in publications. When it was discovered that that time was quite important, techniques were developed to minimize and eliminate the ‘‘dead’’ time. Rangan et al. use VD = 50 mV and monitor the drain current during stress and recovery. Drain current depends on effective mobility and threshold voltage and in this method neither is measured explicitly, but the drain current degradation is determined. Huard et al. in their “on the fly” method, apply a low drain voltage pulse of ~50 mV and record the drain current at the end of the stress period, without interrupting the stress. This was repeated and in this way drain current degradation was recorded with no “dead” time. Since the current depends on threshold voltage, mobility and other device parameters, it is not possible to extract ΔVT. In a modification of this method, small gate voltage pulses are superimposed on the dc gate stress voltage. The drain current is also pulsed and the drain current is measured at three different gate voltages, allowing the transconductance to be determined.

ground_truth_answer:
Wafer orientation significantly influences Negative Bias Temperature Instability (NBTI) degradation in p-MOSFETs. Specifically, (110) oriented silicon wafers exhibit higher hole mobility compared to the conventional (100) oriented wafers when the channel is aligned in the (110) direction. However, this advantage comes with a trade-off: (110) wafers have a higher interface trap density (Dy(110) = 14x10^ cm^2 eV^-1) than (100) wafers (Dy(100) = 7.7x10^ cm^2 eV^-1), which leads to more severe NBTI degradation. This presents a potential reliability concern if (110) wafers become widely used. Additionally, in three-dimensional devices like FinFETs fabricated on (100) wafers with channels oriented in the (110) direction, the vertical sidewalls are (110) oriented, also causing NBTI issues. Conversely, aligning channels along the (100) direction results in (100) vertical sidewalls, mitigating this problem.

NBTI characterization typically involves stressing the device and interrupting the stress to measure parameters such as threshold voltage (VT), drain current (ID), effective channel length (leff), interface trap density (Dit), and oxide trapped charge (Not). Common measurement techniques include ID–VD and ID–VG electrical characterizations for VT, ID, and leff, and charge pumping or sub-threshold swing measurements for Dit. Traditionally, there has been a 'dead' time between stress and measurement, which can introduce artifacts and affect accuracy. To minimize these effects, advanced methods have been developed. For example, Rangan et al. monitor the drain current continuously during stress and recovery at a low drain voltage (VD = 50 mV), allowing degradation tracking without explicit VT extraction. Huard et al. proposed an 'on the fly' method where a low drain voltage pulse (~50 mV) is applied and the drain current is recorded immediately at the end of the stress period without interrupting stress, eliminating dead time. This approach tracks drain current degradation more accurately, although it cannot isolate VT shifts explicitly. Further improvements involve superimposing small gate voltage pulses on the DC gate stress voltage and pulsing the drain current at multiple gate voltages, enabling determination of transconductance and improved characterization of device parameters during stress.
